steps.title: Map out what the text actually does
steps.content: Sentence 1 introduces Claude Shannon as "widely regarded as a foundational figure in information theory." Sentences 2–3 describe his 1948 paper and how it used the concept of a "bit" to measure and transmit information. The final sentence quotes Robert Gallager saying the bit was Shannon's discovery and that "the whole communications revolution has sprung" from it — a statement of historical significance.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice and pick the match
steps.content:
• Choice A — It presents a theoretical concept, illustrates how the name of the concept has changed, and shows how the name has entered common usage.: The text only briefly notes in parentheses that "binary digit" was shortened to "bit." It never discusses name changes or common usage as a main thread. ✗
  • Choice B — It introduces a respected researcher, describes an aspect of his work, and suggests why the work is historically significant.: Matches exactly: Shannon is introduced as foundational, his paper and the "bit" concept are described, and Gallager's quote conveys historical significance. ✓
  • Choice C — It names the company where an important mathematician worked, details the mathematician's career at the company, and provides an example of the recognition he received there.: Bell Labs is mentioned only briefly; the text does not detail Shannon's career there or recognition he received at Bell Labs. ✗
  • Choice D — It mentions a paper, offers a summary of the paper's findings, and presents a researcher's commentary on the paper.: The text opens with Shannon's reputation, not the paper — the paper comes second. And Gallager was a colleague, not a researcher commenting on the paper specifically. ✗
That is the correct answer.
